---
title: "Methodology"
subtitle: "How the charts are computed, in the smallest number of steps that preserve honesty."
---

## Guiding principle

This site is a **measurement** project, not a causal one. It does not claim the
2026 Iran-US war *caused* any particular move in the charts; it shows what the
most transparent public indicators did over a window in which the war is the
dominant news event. Everything on the main page is derivable from the data
committed to the [repository](https://github.com/pmxlr8/DS_R_Geopolitics).

## Intellectual framing

The framing borrows openly from the DHL Initiative on Globalization's
[Global Connectedness Index](https://www.stern.nyu.edu/experience-stern/about/departments-centers-initiatives/centers-of-research/center-future-management/dhl-initiative-globalization)
(Ghemawat & Altman). The real index aggregates four flows — trade, capital,
information, people — into a *depth* score and a *breadth* score. Here we use
a simplified depth-of-trade score so the full computation fits on one page:

$$
\text{depth}_{c,t} = \frac{\text{exports}_{c,t} + \text{imports}_{c,t}}{\text{GDP}_{c,t}}
$$

computed directly from World Bank WDI indicator
`NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS`.

## The shock score

For each country we compute a baseline over the 2018–2022 window:

$$
\mu_c = \overline{\text{depth}_{c, 2018..2022}}, \quad
\sigma_c = \text{sd}(\text{depth}_{c, 2018..2022})
$$

and score the latest WDI observation as a z-score:

$$
z_{c,t} = \frac{\text{depth}_{c,t} - \mu_c}{\sigma_c}
$$

A positive z-score means the country is trading *more* relative to GDP than its
2018–2022 normal; a negative score means structural pullback. The implementation
is ~30 lines of R in [`R/connectedness.R`](https://github.com/pmxlr8/DS_R_Geopolitics/blob/main/R/connectedness.R).

**Why 2018–2022?** The window is intentionally chosen to end *before* the
second-order effects of the 2022 Ukraine shock begin to dominate annual trade
statistics. Using 2020–2024 would embed a known shock inside the baseline and
compress any new shock's z-score. 2018–2022 is the most recent five-year window
that anchors on a relatively stable global-trade regime.

## Oil prices

Spot prices come from FRED:

- `DCOILWTICO` — WTI, daily
- `DCOILBRENTEU` — Europe Brent, daily

We fetch them as CSV from the public `fredgraph.csv` endpoint — no API key, no
rate limits that matter at our volume. The Brent-WTI spread is simply daily
difference after pivoting the series wide.

## Event annotations

The war timeline lives in `data/events.yml`. Every entry has a date, a category
(`kinetic`, `markets`, `diplomacy`, `sanctions`, `macro`), a short label, a
detail, and a source. Keeping the timeline in YAML rather than embedding it
inline makes each war milestone a reviewable pull request.

## Data warehouse

Everything lands in a single DuckDB file (`data/shock.duckdb`). DuckDB was
chosen over SQLite because (a) the project is analytical — group-bys, window
functions, joins over ~20k rows — and DuckDB is ~10–100× faster for that
workload, (b) it reads Parquet natively if we ever grow, and (c) a single-file
warehouse commits cleanly and fits the CI model perfectly.

## What this project *cannot* tell you

- Causal attribution. Trade and GDP data are annual and lagged; by the time we
  see 2026 in WDI, the war will be history. For daily impact, the oil spread
  is the sharpest indicator we have.
- Capital, information, or people flows. The real DHL index spans all four;
  this is a trade-only simplification.
- Sub-country detail. Iran's shadow fleet, Chinese teapot refineries, and the
  interior economics of Gulf transshipment are important stories that need
  different data sources than the free public APIs this pipeline runs on.
